# Clinically colorimetric diagnostics of blood glucose levels based on vanadium oxide quantum dots enzyme mimics


Weishuai Ma a,b , Jing Liu a , Ying Xin c , Xuecheng Yang a, ⁎ , Ronggui Li b, ⁎ , Xiaoteng Ding a,b , Yusheng Niu d , Yuanhong Xu a,b, ⁎

- a Department of Urology, Key Laboratory of Urinary System Diseases, Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266003, PR China

b College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao266071, PR China

c Department of Endocrinology and Metabolism, Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao266003, PR China

d School of Tourism and Geography Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao266071, PR China


# A R T I C L E I N F O



# Keywords:


Vanadium oxide quantum dots Peroxidase-like activity Kinetic study Detection of H2O2and glucose


# 1. Introduction


As a metabolic intermediate and important energy source of living cells, glucose plays an indispensable role in human health [1-3], but excessive blood glucose levels in the body could cause diabetes and eventually lead to complications such as heart disease [4,5]. Therefore, the glucose detection is particularly important in clinical diagnosis and therapy. Peroxidase, as a type of oxidoreductase, has been widely applied in developing colorimetric detection kits of glucose [6]. However, because of the complexity of the purification process [7], high cost [8] and easy deterioration [9], the widespread use of natural peroxidase was greatly limited. With the development of nanomaterials with enzyme-like catalytic properties, nanozymes were proposed and expected to be superior replacement of the natural ones [10,11].

Among the peroxidase-like nanozymes, vanadium oxide (VOx)based ones such as nanofibers, nanosheets and nanorods have been attracting increasing attention recently due to their advantages of high

⁎ Corresponding Authors

E-mail addresses:

yangxc@qduhospital.cn (X. Yang), lrg@qdu.edu.cn (R. Li), yhxu@qdu.edu.cn (Y. Xu).


# A B S T R A C T


The environmental vulnerability of the natural enzyme have always been the most concerned issue in the diagnosis of blood glucose levels, which could be settled by the discovery and application of nanozyme mimics. However, the nanozyme still encountered with the problem of complex preparation process or low enzymatic activities. Herein, peroxidase-like vanadium oxide quantum dots (VOxQDs) were synthesized via a one-step bottom-up solvothermal method using vanadium (Ⅲ) trichloride (VCl3) powder as the precursor. Besides, the most favorable temperature and solvent for VOxQDs synthesis were confirmed. Due to the inherent properties of quantum dots, including larger specific surface areas and more active sites, the as-prepared VOxQDs exhibited43times lower value of Km and32times higher one of Vmax than those of natural peroxidase using H2O2as the substrate, indicating the higher affinity and stronger peroxidase-like activity of the VOxQDs nanozymes. Accordingly, a facile and sensitive colorimetric sensor to detect glucose was designed via the integrated enzyme system of VOxQDs and glucose oxidase, which showed wider linear range of0.005 - 2mM glucose and lower limit of detection of1.7 μM compared with those of many other peroxidase-like nanozymes. Especially, the colorimetric sensor was demonstrated with reliable and satisfactory performance in detection of glucose in human serum, which was in good accordance with the clinical results provided from the domestic hospital.

chemical stability [12,13], abundant resources and low cost [14-16]. Meanwhile, it was reported that the catalytic capability would be determined by the specific surface area and active sites of the nanozymes [17,18]. Therein, the zero-dimensional quantum dots (QDs) have drawn researchers' special attention due to their edge and size effects, which is in accordance with the smaller size, larger specific surface area to expose more active sites [12,19,24,25]. Accordingly, VOxQDs were confirmed with higher enzyme-like catalytic activity than that of their bulk or large forms [20,21]. Thereby enhanced sensitivity and expanded detection range could be expected based on such nanozymebased sensing system. However, there were no reports about using enzyme-like VOxQDs for diagnostics of blood glucose levels.

In this work, the peroxidase mimetic VOxQDs prepared via a typical hydrothermal/solvothermal method was explored for establishing a facile and sensitive colorimetric sensor for glucose detection. The dependence of the morphology on the solvent and temperature was studied in detail based on the transmission electron microscopy (TEM),

fluorescence measurements, UV-vis spectroscopy, electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements, etc. More importantly, the practicality of the proposed sensor was tested for detecting blood glucose levels in clinical serum.


# 2. Experimental section



# 2.1. Materials


O-phenylenediamine (OPD), terephthalic acid (TA), 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and dimethyl pyridine N-oxide (DMPO) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd., China. glucose oxidase (GOx) and horseradish peroxidase (HRP, >250u/mg) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd. Glucose, ethanol, VCl3and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. All reagents were of analytical grade and applied as received.


# 2.2. Apparatus


TEM was carried out on a JEOL JEM-2100microscope instrument at acceleration voltage of200kV. The PL spectra were measured on a FS5-TCSPC-1189-0317-A3732-1spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, United Kingdom). The UV absorption spectra were recorded on a Mapada UV-1800PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China), respectively.


# 2.3. Preparation of the VOxQDs


The VOxQDs were synthesized directly via a one-pot solvothermal method using either DI water or ethanol as solvent and VCl3as precursor. Moreover, to obtain VOxQDs with uniform distribution and similar size, the reaction conditions were optimized. The typical procedure was as below: Firstly, VCl3 (0.2g) was added with20mL solvent and thoroughly mixed. Secondly, the mixture was ultrasonic treatment at200W power for15min and then was respectively heated at120 °C, 140 °C, 160 °C, 180 °C and200 °C for10h, respectively. Then the mixture was cooled naturally and centrifuged at12000rpm for10min. Finally, the VOxQDs was obtained by collecting the supernatant.


# 2.4. Investigation of the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the VOxQDs


To study the peroxidase-mimic property of the as-prepared VOxQDs, TMB and H2O2were chosen as the substrates to detect the catalytic oxidation. In short, the whole reaction system was200 μL in the acetate buffer (100mM, pH3.0), which contained1mM TMB, 200 μg mL -1VOxQDs and various concentrations of H2O2. The whole system was thoroughly mixed and incubated at30 °C for30min, then the absorbance of the reaction color (at652nm for TMB) was recorded by a microplate reader.


# 2.5. Kinetics analysis for peroxidase-mimic activity of the VOxQDs


Kinetics of the nanozyme were carried out in the acetate buffer (100mM, pH3.0) with the final concentration of TMB (1mM) and diverse concentrations of H2O2 (0.005-0.15mM) or with the final concentration of H2O2 (0.3mM) and various concentration of TMB (0.01-0. 9mM). The values of Km and Vmax were measured by the following equation:

where V, Vmax, [S] and Km stand for the initial maximal reaction velocity, the concentration of the reaction substrate and the Michaelis constant [22], respectively.


# 2.6. ESR measurements


The ESR measurements were detected using X-band Bruker E-500EPR spectrometer (Karlsruhe, Germany) at room temperature. 1G field modulation, 100G scan range, and200mW microwave power was used to detected the spin adducts. The detection of hydroxyl radical ( · OH) was carried out in the presence of the following conditions containing10mM DMPO, 100mM H2O2and200 μg mL -1VOxQDs, which were completely mixed for10min before measurement. The above experimental operation was performed in the acetate buffer (100mM, pH3.0).


# 2.7. Fluoresence measurement of the · OH


The non-fluorescent TA was applied as the fluorescent probe, which could combines with · OH to generate highly fluorescent product (TAOH) at435nm [23]. In brief, 100mM H2O2, 200 μg mL -1VOxQDs and500mM TA were added to phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH = 7.4, 25mM). The mixture reacted for12h at room temperature and the fluorescence spectra were measured using the spectrofluorometer subsequently.


# 2.8. Colorimetric detection of H2O2


H2O2detection was carried out in the presence of1mM TMB, 200 μg mL -1VOxQDs in the acetate buffer (200mM, pH3.0). Different H2O2concentrations was tested including0.5 μM, 0.7 μM, 1.0 μM, 5.0 μM, 10.0 μM, 20.0 μM, 30.0 μM, 40.0 μM, 50.0 μM and0.1mM. After reacting under the optimum conditions, the absorbance of the suspension was recorded at652nm via a microplate reader. The control experiments were performed using acetate buffer without H2O2as the blank. The linear relationship was plotted between the H2O2concentration and the absorbance. The limit of detection (LOD) was measured at S/N = 3.0.


# 2.9. Colorimetric detection of glucose


The experiment of glucose detection was performed as follows: glucose with various concentrations (0.005-4mM) was added into PBS (200mM, pH7.4) containing GOx (2mg mL -1 ). After being incubated at37 °C for30min, the mixture suspension was collected and added with acetate buffer (200mM, pH3.0) to stop the reaction. Soon afterwards, 1mM TMB and200 μg mL -1VOxQDs were subsequently added into the mixture suspension and incubated in30 °C for30min, the absorbance at652nm was recorded via a microplate reader. The linear relationship was fitted with the glucose concentration against the absorbance. In addition, the specificity tests were carried out by using maltose, lactose, D-galactose, sucrose, and D-(+)-xylose all at0.5mM. The detection process was consistent with the standard glucose assay.


# 2.10. Evaluation of the practicability for the clinically colorimetric glucose assay


To investigate the practicability of the VOxQDs in clinical testing, four human serums were prepared in the following experiment. These clinical samples were provided by the Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University. All the serum samples were obtained by centrifugation at3000rpm for10min, subsequently assayed via the established GOxVOxQDs system using the same procedure as the standard glucose detection. After adding50 μL GOx stock solution into150 μL of the serum, the as-obtained samples were incubated at37 °C for30min, respectively. Later on, the as-prepared serums with a certain dilution was added with1mM TMB and200 μg mL -1VOxQDs in the acetate buffer (200mM, pH = 3.0) to reach the total volume of the mixed suspension of200 μL. Finally, the mixed suspension was incubated at30 °C for30min. The corresponding absorbance was detected via a microplate

Fig. 1. The TEM images of VCl3treated with one-pot template/surfactant-free hydrothermal method at120 °C (A), 140 °C (B), 160 °C (C) and180 °C (D) for10h, respectively. (E) The particle size distribution. (F) The UV-vis absorption spectrum, maximum excitation (EX) and emission (EM) in the PL spectra. The inserted image shows color changes of the as-prepared VOxQDs under visible light (left) and UV irradiation at365nm (right).

reader. The glucose concentrations of samples were calculated based on the standard curve method. The obtained results were compared with those determined using the results provided from the hospital.


# 3. Results and discussion



# 3.1. Characterization of the as-obtained VOxQDs


Fig. 1A-D and Figure S1shows the TEM images of the as-prepared VOxQDs prepared via the solvothermal method at120 °C, 140 °C, 160 °C, 180 °C and200 °C for10h, respectively. As shown, with the increasing temperature, the as-prepared products exhibited decreasing size. In addition, the particle distribution showed not only nanosize dots but also good mono-dispersibility upon the treatment at180 °C. The average diameter of the VOxQDs was3.39 ± 0.57nm by statistics of the100particles (Fig. 1E). In addition, higher temperature of200 °C wouldn't result in the further significant change in particle size and distribution. The photos both under the daylight and UV irradiation showed that the as-obtained nanodots at180 °C exhibited highly bright blue emission, indicating the most typically characteristics of quantum dots for the corresponding as-prepared products (inset in the Fig. 1F). In addition, the solvent used in the synthesis was optimized. As shown in Figure S2, when DI water was applied as the solvent, the as-prepared product showed either poor dispersibility or large size of30-40nm at180 °C or200 °C, respectively. Meanwhile, the poor light emission under UV irradiation was obtained, indicating the low fluorescence properties of the corresponding as-obtained products. Due to the size effects, the smaller diameter with uniform size distribution was expected with larger specific surface area as well as more active sites. Accordingly, 180 °C and ethanol were selected as the most favorable temperature and solvent for VOxQDs synthesis. The typical UV-vis absorption and fluorescence measurement were applied to study the optical features of the VOxQDs. As can be seen from Fig. 1F, the typical UV-vis absorption located at280nm. Meanwhile, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were found at340nm and430nm, respectively [26,27]. All the above results confirmed the successful preparation of the VOxQDs, which were consistent with the previous works [21].


# 3.2. Peroxidase-mimic activity of the as-prepared VOxQDs


To explore whether the as-prepared VOxQDs possess peroxidase activity, we designed various reaction systems of catalytic oxidation taking TMB as chromogenic substrate. As shown in Fig. 2A, VOxQDs could catalyze the TMB oxidation within H2O2, showing a blue color reaction with a distinct absorption peak at652nm. There were no significant color changes in the control groups such as the ones of the H2O2either in the presence of TMB (curve c) or VOxQDs (curve d) alone, indicating that the reaction must be carried out based on both two components. Meanwhile, the absorbance of the system (VOxQDs + TMB + H2O2) at652nm increased with the increasing H2O2concentration (Fig. 2B). The dependence of the peroxidase-mimic activity on the concentrations of VOxQDs has also been investigated (Fig. 3). As shown, with the increasing content of VOxQDs, the peroxidase activity of VOxQDs enhanced accordingly. It suggested that the enzyme-like activity of the tested system (VOxQDs + TMB + H2O2) was determined by the concentration of the VOxQDs. All these results clearly proved the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of VOxQDs. In addition, various peroxidase substrates including OPD and ABTS were used to additionally confirm the peroxidase-like features of the VOxQDs. Blue, green and orange solution corresponding to adsorption peaks at652nm, 416nm and450nm, respectively, were obtained, which corresponding to the characteristically oxidation peak of TMB [28], ABTS [17] and OPD [29], respectively (Fig. 2C). All of the results proved that the as-prepared VOxQDs could be regarded as a highly efficient peroxidase mimic, which could generally produce · OH by decomposing H2O2.

Accordingly, ESR measurements was carried out to verify the formation of the · OH using DMPO as the radical capturer. As indicated in Fig. 2D, a typical spectrum of · OH with strength ratio of1:2:2:1was

presented in the presence of VOxQDs, while there was no significant spectrum for the control without VOxQDs [30]. Meanwhile, the formation of the · OH could be further confirmed by the TA-based fluorescence experiments. The non-fluorescent TA could easily combine with a · OH to generate a highly fluorescent product (TAOH) at435nm [23]. As indicated in Fig. 2E and2F, significant fluorescence enhancement was observed in the group of VOxQDs + TMB + H2O2, while no fluorescence enhancement was observed in other control groups. All of the above results were indicative of the presence of · OH during the decomposition of H2O2.


# 3.3. Kinetic studies of catalytic reactivity for the as-prepared VOxQDs


To explore the reaction mechanism of the peroxidase activity of VOxQDs, the steady-state kinetic assay was carried out in acetate buffer (100mM, pH3.0) at30 °C. The slopes of the initial velocity were determined by the absorbance-time curve for TMB derived oxidation

Fig. 2. (A) The UV-vis absorption spectrum of various reaction systems different mixtures in the acetate buffer (100mM, pH3.0) at30 °C for30min: (a) VOxQDs+TMB+H2O2; (b) VOxQDs+TMB; (c) TMB+H2O2; (d) VOxQDs+H2O2. The concentration of VOxQDs, H2O2and TMB were200 μg mL-1, 1mM and1mM, respectively. (B) The UVVis absorption spectrum of VOxQDs + TMB within different concentrations of H2O2. (C) Various substrates were oxidized to produce different color reactions by the POD-like catalysis. (a) TMB, (b) ABTS, (c) OPD. (D) ESR characterization of the generation of the ·OH during the POD-like catalysis. (E) Fluorescence spectra obtained from various reaction systems. (F) The normalized ∆PL intensity of the TA fluorescent probe with or without VOxQDs.

products. The kinetic parameters were calculated with the Michaelis-Menten curves by changing one substrate concentration while fixing another one. As shown in Figure S3A and B, there was an obvious dependence between initial reaction rates and substrate concentration. Furthermore, to investigate the catalytic reaction of the VOxQDs, the Lineweaver-Burk plot method was performed. As can be seen from Figure S3C and3D, the reciprocal of the initial rate was positively correlated with the reciprocal of the substrate concentration. The kinetic parameters (Km and Vmax) were obtained and compared with those for some other peroxidase mimetics including Pd@Pt [31], CdS NPs [32], Zn-CuO [33] and Co3O4NPs [34], respectively. As shown in Table S1, the Km value of the VOxQDs using H2O2as a substrate was0.0843mM, which was43times lower than HRP and also lower than other peroxidase mimetics, indicating that the VOxQDs had stronger affinity to H2O2 [22]. Furthermore, the Vmax value of the VOxQDs using TMB as a substrate was2.73 × 10 -6M s -1 , which was27times higher than HRP and also higher than other peroxidase mimetics, indicating

Fig. 3. (A) VOxQDs concentration-dependent peroxidase-like activity with TMB (1mM) and H2O2 (1mM) and (B) the corresponding spectra.

that the VOxQDs had higher peroxidase-like activity toward the catalytic reaction [35], which may be due to the inherent characteristics of quantum dots with more active catalytic sites and larger specific surface area [12].


# 3.4. Colorimetric detection of H2O2


Base on the enzyme-like activity of the VOxQDs, the colorimetric determination of H2O2was carried out conveniently at the optimum pH (pH3.0) and temperature (30 °C). The calibration curve is drawn based on the absorbance at652nm as a function of H2O2concentration (Fig. 4A). The linear range was0.5-100 μM with the regression equation of y = 0.00713x + 0.18125 ( R2 =0.9923), which was wider than that of the other nanozymes such as H2TCPP-Co3O4 (1-75 μM) [36] and glycine-MIL-53(Fe) (0.1-10 μM) [37]. Furthermore, the LOD was estimated to be0.42 μM, which was lower than that based on Fe3O4MNPs (0.5 μM) [38]. All of the above results revealed that the as-prepared

Fig. 4. H2O2and glucose assays based on the peroxidase-mimicf VOxQDs. (A) The UV-Vis absorbance of the VOxQDs + TMB system with various concentrations of H2O2and (B) the corresponding spectra (the concentration of H2O2was0.5 μM, 0.7 μM, 1 μM, 5 μM, 0.01mM, 0.02mM, 0.03mM, 0.04mM, 0.05mM, 0.1mM and0.3mM, respectively). (C) Calibration curve for glucose detection. (D) Specificity analysis of the glucose detection. The error bars represent the standard deviation for three measurements.

VOxQDs could be used to detect H2O2with high sensitivity and wide detection range.


# 3.5. Colorimetric assays of glucose and assessment of practicability


The standard glucose assay was based on the double enzyme (GOxVOxQDs) system to detect glucose. As shown in Fig. 5, due to the catalytic oxidation activity of GOx, glucose was decomposed to generate gluconic acid and H2O2. Then, the formed H2O2could be catalyzed based on the peroxidase-like activity of the VOxQDs to achieve colorimetric glucose assay. The linear range for glucose was0.005 - 2mM with the regression equation of y = 1.72622x + 0.28117 (R2 =0.991) (Fig.. 4C), which was wider than those based on the V2O3 - OMC (0.01 4mM) [39], V2O5nanozymes (0.01-2mM) [40], Pt-MoO3 (0.005 0.5mM) [41] and Fe3O4@MSNPs (0.01-0.5mM) [38] based colorimetric detection of glucose. In Table S2, the LOD of glucose was calculated to be1.7 μM, which was lower than that based on the Fe3O4

Fig. 5. Schematic illustration of colorimetric determination of glucose using GOx-VOxQDs colorimetric system.

@MSNPs (4.0 μM) [38], V2O5nanozymes (10 μM) [40], VO2nanofibers (18 μM) [42] and Co3O4NPs (5 μM) [43], etc.

To evaluate the selectivity of the colorimetric sensor, other sugars including maltose, lactose, D-galactose, sucrose, and D-(+)-xylose all at0.5mM were tested to conduct the control experiment under the same conditions of glucose. Compared with the dark blue and high absorbance value, the control groups showed either light blue or colorless solution, which confirmed that the double enzyme (GOx-VOxQDs) system has excellent specificity for detecting glucose.

To confirm the practicability of the proposed VOxQDs nanozymesbased colorimetric sensor, it was used for the visual detection of glucose content in four different human serums from local hospital. The four serum samples in the presence and absence of VOxQDs and TMB were shown in Fig. 6A-D, respectively. Accordingly, the detection results were summarized in Table S3. As compared, the results obtained by this present method were basically consistent with the clinical data from the domestic hospital, indicating that the method had good accuracy and reliability for the detection of glucose in clinical samples.


# 4. Conclusions


In summary, we have successfully prepared VOxQDs by a one-step solvothermal method using VCl3powder as the precursor. The most favorable temperature and solvent for VOxQDs synthesis was confirmed. According to the analysis of the TEM data, it could be concluded that the average lateral diameter of VOxQDs were3.39 ± 0.57nm. Kinetic studies indicated that the as-prepared VOxQDs had excellent affinity towards H2O2that the Km value was0.0843mM, which was43times lower than that of HRP. On this basis, a highly sensitive and selective colorimetric sensor to detect H2O2and glucose was designed. The limit of detection was estimated to be0.42and1.7 μM for H2O2and glucose, respectively. The proposed colorimetric sensor was successfully applied to clinical serum testing. All of our results demonstrated the as-prepared VOxQDs possessed great potential applications in bio-detection and clinical detection.
